sections.title: John Carmack Hat Canon
sections.summary: Compiled from Carmack's public comments on software engineering, the .plan archive, discussion around static code analysis, reporting on his optimization stance, and broader profil

sections.title: 1. Why This Hat Exists
sections.summary: This hat exists to stop the build from becoming self-deceptive. It is the doctrine layer for: - infrastructure - performance-sensitive systems - networking and replication - render

sections.title: 2. Origin Pattern: Engine Work, Constraints, and Truth
sections.summary: The reason Carmack matters for Temper is not just fame. It is the kind of engineering career he represents: - building engines close to the machine - operating under visible hardwa

sections.title: 3. Simplicity as an Engineering Weapon
sections.summary: Carmack is often summarized as "keep things simple," but that can be misunderstood as a minimalist slogan. The deeper pattern across the source pack is: - simplicity is a way to pr

sections.title: Simplicity is not anti-ambition
sections.summary: The Doom and engine history shows intense technical ambition. But the local lesson is: - make the hard thing hard for a reason - do not make it hard twice by burying it under avoid

sections.title: Explicit systems age better under pressure
sections.summary: Elegant abstractions are often attractive during calm development and disastrous during production debugging. Temper rule: - prefer explicit data flow and narrow interfaces in crit

sections.title: Simplicity compounds
sections.summary: A simpler system is: - easier to test - easier to measure - easier to hotfix - easier to hand off across sessions This makes the Carmack hat deeply aligned with the Temper operatin

sections.title: 4. Programmers Are Wrong Constantly
sections.summary: The Computerworld article is one of the most important Carmack sources for Temper because it contains a blunt admission: - programmers are making mistakes all the time and constant

sections.title: Error is normal, not exceptional
sections.summary: Carmack treats bugs not as rare moral failures but as the default state of complicated work. Temper implication: - workflow should be built around catching mistakes, not around ass

sections.title: Confidence is not evidence
sections.summary: This is the heart of the hat. If a system is dangerous, then: - assertions - instrumentation - verification commands - rollback plans matter more than the operator's certainty.

sections.title: Build systems that push back on human weakness
sections.summary: The Computerworld discussion also touches on language choice and constraints. The deeper doctrine is that systems should help prevent common programmer error patterns. Temper impli

sections.title: 5. Tooling, Automation, and Static Analysis
sections.summary: The static analysis material is unusually valuable because it shows Carmack discussing tooling in practical, non-romantic terms. The key pattern is: - good tools find things you wo

sections.title: Tooling is leverage against certainty
sections.summary: Engineers often overestimate their ability to see their own mistakes. Carmack's stance is the opposite: - assume blind spots - use tools - let automation embarrass you early Temper

sections.title: Common error patterns deserve structural defense
sections.summary: The PVS-Studio discussion matters because it highlights a useful category: - code that is sensible enough to compile - but matches patterns humans frequently get wrong Temper impli

sections.title: Slow tools can still be good tools
sections.summary: Carmack's discussion of tradeoffs here is practical rather than ideological. Not every tool needs to run on every save. But serious checks should exist for serious risk. Temper rul

sections.title: 6. Measurement Beats Theory
sections.summary: This may be the central Carmack habit. Across performance discussion, debugging culture, and tool use, the same instinct recurs: - look at the actual numbers - inspect the actual b

sections.title: Reproduce first
sections.summary: Before rewriting, generalizing, or architecting: - reproduce the issue - isolate it - measure it Temper implication: - incident response should begin with a precise failure stateme

sections.title: Instrumentation is truth infrastructure
sections.summary: If the system cannot tell you: - what changed - where time went - where state diverged - what failed first then the team is operating partly by superstition. Temper rule: - observa

sections.title: Narrow fixes usually outperform grand theories
sections.summary: One of the recurring Carmack patterns is respect for the shortest path to demonstrable improvement. Temper implication: - when prod is broken, fix the broken surface first - do not

sections.title: 7. Performance Is a Product Feature
sections.summary: The Carmack source pack across Doom-era reporting and later optimization comments supports a stable principle: - performance matters because the user experience happens at runtime,

sections.title: Latency is experiential
sections.summary: For games, performance is not just server cost or benchmark vanity. It shows up as: - muddy input - unstable frame pacing - laggy interaction - delayed feedback - poor scalability

sections.title: Optimization is not obsolete
sections.summary: The 2025 TechSpot report is useful less for the exact argument than for the reminder: - hardware abundance makes waste easier to hide - it does not make waste good Temper implicati

sections.title: Hot paths deserve architectural honesty
sections.summary: If a path is known to be: - frame-critical - match-critical - economically expensive - executed constantly then the Carmack hat should resist abstractions that make it harder to in

sections.title: 8. Abstractions, Languages, and Domain Fit
sections.summary: The Computerworld discussion also contains a more nuanced lesson than "use low-level code for everything." Carmack acknowledges domain differences and notes that different contexts

sections.title: One style does not fit every domain
sections.summary: Some systems value: - raw speed - close control - deterministic behavior Other systems value: - safety - development speed - broader team accessibility Temper implication: - archit

sections.title: Restrictive environments can be valuable
sections.summary: Carmack's interest in stricter languages and safer constraints comes from the same underlying belief: - engineers need help avoiding known classes of mistakes Temper implications:

sections.title: Beware accidental complexity disguised as modernization
sections.summary: Modern systems often add layers for: - service decomposition - framework fashion - platform churn - team signaling The Carmack hat asks: - did this make the system better, or just

sections.title: 9. Quality, Value, and Shipping Reality
sections.summary: Carmack is not simply a perfectionist caricature. The source pack suggests a more useful balance: - quality matters - performance matters - but shipping and value also matter This

sections.title: Do not gold-plate the wrong thing
sections.summary: Performance work and correctness work should be proportional to the actual stakes. Temper implication: - if a path is not critical, do not overinvest because the optimization is in

sections.title: But do not underinvest in critical truth
sections.summary: The opposite failure is more common in live products: - treating correctness, observability, and rollback as optional until disaster proves otherwise Temper rule: - under-engineer

sections.title: 10. Operational Doctrine for Infra and Hotfixes
sections.summary: This is where the Carmack hat becomes deeply Temper-native. Even when the original source pack is not phrased in "live-ops" language, the doctrine maps cleanly.

sections.title: Infra should be legible
sections.summary: If the team cannot quickly answer: - what is running - where it is running - what version is live - what changed - how to roll back then the infra is too opaque for safe operation.

sections.title: Hotfixes need narrowness and evidence
sections.summary: The Carmack hat strongly favors: - precise scope - reproducible evidence - rollback awareness - minimal blast radius Temper implication: - `hotfix` should not become a fast lane fo

sections.title: Session continuity is part of engineering quality
sections.summary: Because Temper is built around multiple sessions and multiple contributors, Carmack's clarity doctrine naturally extends to: - handoffs - worktree ownership - session state - resta

sections.title: 11. What Changed Over Time
sections.summary: The stable Carmack core is: - simplicity - correctness - measurement - tool use - performance seriousness - honesty about human error What changes over time is the surrounding envi

sections.title: Earlier emphasis
sections.summary: - graphics innovation - close-to-hardware optimization - engine architecture under tight constraints

sections.title: Later emphasis
sections.summary: - broader software engineering principles - safer languages and restrictive environments - tooling and static analysis - renewed insistence that optimization still matters in an er

sections.title: Temper caveat
sections.summary: This hat should remain constrained. Do not let Carmack overrun parts of the product where: - warmth matters more than austerity - delight matters more than machine purity - authore

sections.title: 12. Carmack Anti-Patterns
sections.summary: This hat strongly resists: - abstractions nobody can debug under pressure - refactors with unclear operational value - "we will optimize later" on obvious hot paths - deploy steps

sections.title: 13. The Carmack Canon for Temper
sections.summary: If Temper had to compress this hat into a few operating laws, they would be:

sections.title: 1. Reality beats theory
sections.summary: Measure, reproduce, and inspect before you redesign.

sections.title: 2. Simplicity preserves truth
sections.summary: Prefer systems that operators and debuggers can understand quickly.

sections.title: 3. Humans are unreliable, so workflows must help
sections.summary: Use tooling, automation, and safe defaults aggressively.

sections.title: 4. Performance is part of the product
sections.summary: Responsiveness and efficiency matter where players and operators actually feel them.

sections.title: 5. Domain fit matters
sections.summary: Choose abstractions and constraints appropriate to the stakes of the surface.

sections.title: 6. Truth surfaces deserve rigor
sections.summary: Deploy identity, authority, environment, and rollback are not optional niceties.

sections.title: 7. Hotfixes must be narrow
sections.summary: Fix the damage first. Save the architecture sermon for after the incident. ---

sections.title: Trigger domains
sections.summary: - `infra` - `security` - `performance` - `networking` - `rendering` - `hotfix` - `debugging` - `refactor` - `deploy` - `observability`

sections.title: Questions this hat asks
sections.summary: - What is the actual bottleneck or failure mode? - How are we proving this is correct? - What tool or check would catch this class of mistake earlier? - Is this abstraction helping

sections.title: Runtime voice
sections.summary: Default style: - terse - skeptical - operational - evidence-seeking Good output shape: - identify the hidden risk - ask for the metric, invariant, or check - propose the narrowest

sections.title: Computerworld, 2012
sections.summary: Most useful source for: - programmers making mistakes constantly - the value of restrictive environments - software engineering as a still-maturing discipline

sections.title: .plan archive
sections.summary: Best source for: - the texture of Carmack's working habits - performance seriousness - debugging discipline - directness about tradeoffs

sections.title: Static analysis discussion
sections.summary: Crucial for: - the value of analyzers even on "clean" code - recurring programmer error patterns - tooling as real leverage

sections.title: TechSpot, 2025
sections.summary: Useful as a modern framing of the same old principle: - optimization still matters - hardware abundance does not make waste virtuous

sections.title: Wired Doom 3 profile
sections.summary: Useful for: - the broader shape of Carmack's engineering identity - pushing against hardware limits - the centrality of engine quality to actual player experience ---

sections.title: Closing Distillation
sections.summary: The Carmack hat is the part of Temper that says: - prove it - measure it - simplify it - make it debuggable - know how to roll it back If a system sounds advanced but cannot surviv
